metadata.title: Why a Stationary State Is Secretly Spinning
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = phase hand / rotating; CRIMSON = probability density / shadow / still
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no separation-of-variables derivation, no superposition beat term, no eigenvalue formalism.

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. A stationary state is called stationary because nothing you can measure about it changes — yet the wave function underneath is spinning the whole time.

card.copy: Why a Stationary State Is Secretly Spinning

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. A stationary state is called stationary because nothing you can measure about it changes — yet the wave function underneath is spinni

beats.narration_text: Measure an electron's position in its ground state — same histogram every time, no matter when you do it. Stationary means: no observable thing changes with time.

Yet the Schrodinger equation demands the wave function change. The time factor is e to the minus i E t over h-bar. That's not constant — it rotates in the complex plane. Why doesn't this change anything?

card.copy: The wave function has a rotating phase. Measurements stay constant. How can a spinning thing cast a still shadow?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Yet the Schrodinger equation demands the wave function change. The time factor is e to the minus i E t over h-bar. That's not constant — it rotates in the compl

beats.narration_text: The wave function is a complex number at each point. Think of it as a clock hand — it has a length and a direction in the complex plane.
In a stationary state, that clock hand spins at a rate set by the energy — E divided by h-bar. Faster energy level, faster rotation.
Now the probability density: it's the length of the clock hand squared. The length doesn't change as it spins. The shadow on the floor holds still.
The clock is the wave function. The shadow is |psi| squared. The wave function spins; the probability density doesn't. Stationary means the shadow, not the clock.

This is why energy eigenstates are special: their probabilities are time-independent even though the wave functions aren't. Mix two eigenstates and the rotation rates differ — then the shadow starts to move.

Illustrative example: a ground state with energy 1 eV. Its phase rotates at about 1.5 times 10 to the 15 turns per second — a million times faster than visible light oscillates. The spin is real, just undetectable by itself.
The wave function is a spinning clock. The probability density is its shadow on the floor. The shadow holds still. The clock never stops.

card.copy: The wave function spins. The probability is its shadow. The shadow holds still.
